text.title: best-practices-2026 Core Rules
text.summary: Eight testable rules covering AI-assisted coding, TypeScript 5 strict, React 19/Next 15, Python 3.12/3.13, and AI-testing discipline as of mid-2026.

rule.statement: Match the AI coding tool to the task. Copilot â€” daily inline completion in the IDE. Cursor â€” exploratory multi-file edits with chat. Claude Code â€” documentation, large refactors, test-suite generation, agentic loops. Using one tool for all tasks degrades output.
rule.rationale: Each tool has a different context window, interaction model, and latency profile. The cost of mismatched tooling is wasted prompts and worse code.

rule.statement: Structure every coding prompt with four parts: Context (stack, constraints, target file), Task (specific action), Requirements (must-haves, edge cases), Output (expected format, file locations). Unstructured prompts are forbidden in tracked sessions.
rule.rationale: Unstructured prompts produce generic code that ignores project conventions. The four-part structure anchors the model to specifics.

rule.statement: Never auto-accept AI-generated code for auth, data access, payments, or business-critical logic. Manual line-by-line review required. Tools may auto-apply diffs only in `tests/`, `docs/`, and stylistic refactors.
rule.rationale: AI can increase defect rates significantly without review on security-sensitive surfaces.

rule.statement: Enable `strict: true` PLUS `noUncheckedIndexedAccess`, `exactOptionalPropertyTypes`, `verbatimModuleSyntax`, and `moduleResolution: "bundler"` in every new TypeScript project.
rule.rationale: These flags together catch the most common runtime errors at compile time. The plain `strict` preset misses array-index-OOB and excess-property edge cases.
rule.reference: Working tsconfig with the flags above.

rule.statement: On React 19+/Next 15+: prefer Server Components for data-fetching surfaces; use `use(promise)` only inside Suspense boundaries; use `'use client'` directives sparingly (per file). Do not introduce `getServerSideProps`-era idioms in new code.
rule.rationale: RSC reduces client bundle size and shifts data-fetching closer to data sources. The deprecated SSR data hooks still work but are no longer the recommended path on App Router.

rule.statement: Pin Python floor at 3.12 (3.13 next-version target). Use PEP 695 type parameter syntax for new generics. Use `tomllib` stdlib for TOML reads. Use `pathlib.Path` over `os.path`. `pyproject.toml` is the single config file â€” no `setup.cfg`, no `.flake8`.
rule.rationale: 3.12+ is the supported floor across major Python ecosystems (Django 5.x, FastAPI). 3.13 brings the experimental free-threaded build but is not yet default-on.

rule.statement: AI-generated tests are drafts, not oracles. Every AI-drafted test MUST be reviewed against the production code it claims to verify. Property-based testing (Hypothesis, fast-check) and mutation testing (mutmut, Stryker) complement but do not replace AI-drafted unit tests.

rationale.source: 2025 study: AI-generated tests passed at compile-time but missed mutation 38% of the time

rule.rationale: AI tests routinely encode the implementation, not the contract. Mutation testing is the cheapest reality check.

rule.statement: Extract the relevant rules into the project's `constitution.md`; cite `best-practices-2026` once with the snapshot version. Re-run the drift scanner quarterly. Rules that no longer match current-stable practice are deprecated, not edited in place.
rule.rationale: This file is a snapshot and will age. The constitution is the versioned, project-specific contract agents cite without risking stale references.
